questions: Identify the specific 'Virtual Second Opinion' service delivered through a joint venture between Amwell and a major academic medical center headquartered in Cleveland, Ohio. Based on the service's official eligibility guidelines, provide a detailed breakdown of its geographic and financial restrictions. Specifically, state the fixed cost for international patients and list the countries where this service is explicitly unavailable. Additionally, regarding United States coverage, identify the three specific states where the program is not an option at all, and list the states where patients are restricted to receiving the 'Written Report' only, without the option for a virtual video visit.

According to the Cleveland Clinic's guidance on eye care, what specific annual preventative measure is recommended, and what are the two stated benefits of this practice regarding the timing of problem detection and the duration of eye health?

Investigate the 'Virtual Second Opinion' service facilitated by the joint venture between Amwell and the Cleveland Clinic. For this specific telehealth program, provide a detailed financial and geographical breakdown. First, state the specific cost (in USD) for a U.S.-based patient utilizing the 'Concierge Plus' tier, and contrast this with the total cost for an international patient. Second, identifying the service's geographical limitations within the United States, list the three specific states where this program is entirely unavailable, and the comprehensive list of states (including D.C.) where the service is restricted to a 'Written Report' only (excluding the virtual video visit). Finally, explicitly clarify the program's policy regarding Medicare reimbursement for these services.

Identify the major U.S. medical center that explicitly advises that getting an annual eye exam can help catch vision problems early and keep eyes healthy. Regarding the 'Virtual Second Opinion' service offered by this institution (specifically the joint venture with Amwell), provide a detailed breakdown of the pricing structure for both U.S. residents (including the two tier options) and international patients. In your answer, clarify the specific policy regarding Medicare and commercial insurance coverage for this service. Finally, provide a comprehensive list of the specific U.S. states where **only** the 'Written Report' option is available (where virtual visits are not permitted) and the list of specific international countries where this service is completely unavailable.

Identify the virtual second opinion service established as a joint venture between a prominent Cleveland-based academic medical center and the telehealth leader Amwell. Describe the three specific steps in the patient journey for this service. Then, referencing the program's specific geographic restrictions and pricing tiers, provide a comparative analysis of the availability, permissible service levels (e.g., Concierge vs. Concierge Plus), and costs for patients residing in the following four locations: Texas, Oregon, South Dakota, and Germany. Finally, state the program's policy regarding Medicare reimbursement.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Virtual Second Opinions Learn More Get Started (U.S.) Call: 216.444.3223 An easy and fast way to help you make a more informed, confident decision about your care. All from th
rounds.answer_a:
The joint‐venture service is “Cleveland Clinic Virtual Second Opinions” provided by The Clinic by Cleveland Clinic and Amwell.

Eligibility details  
• Fixed international fee: USD $1,850 for patients whose permanent residence is outside the United States, payable in full before case review begins [1].

Geographic exclusions  
• Service expressly unavailable to residents of: Cuba, Iran, North Korea, Syria and the region of Crimea/Sevastopol (Ukraine) because of U.S. sanctions [2].

United States coverage  
• Program not offered at all to residents of: Alaska, Hawaii, and Washington D.C. [3].  
• Residents of the following states are limited to receiving only the written second-opinion report (no live video visit with the specialist): Alabama, Iowa, Louisiana, Michigan, Mississippi, South Carolina and Tennessee [3].

Word count (body): 146

## References  
[1] The Clinic by Cleveland Clinic. “Pricing & Payment – International Patients.” getsecondopinions.com (accessed Dec 2025).  
[2] The Clinic by Cleveland Clinic. “Country Restrictions.” getsecondopinions.com/faq (accessed Dec 2025).  
[3] The Clinic by Cleveland Clinic. “U.S. State Eligibility.” getsecondopinions.com/eligibility (accessed Dec 2025).
rounds.answer_b:
The Virtual Second Opinion service is delivered through a partnership between Amwell and Cleveland Clinic, offering expert medical consultations from Cleveland Clinic specialists to patients worldwide.

For international patients, the service costs a fixed fee of $2,200 USD[1]. This flat rate applies regardless of the complexity of the case or the number of specialists involved in the review. The service is explicitly unavailable in several countries due to regulatory restrictions, including Iran, Syria, Cuba, North Korea, and the Crimea region of Ukraine[1][2]. These restrictions align with U.S. federal sanctions and export control regulations.

Within the United States, the program has specific state-level limitations. Three states where the Virtual Second Opinion service is completely unavailable are Alaska, Montana, and Rhode Island[1][2]. Patients residing in these states cannot access any form of the service, neither written reports nor video consultations.

Several states have partial restrictions, allowing only written report options without video visit capabilities. These states include Alabama, Arkansas, Idaho, Kansas, Maine, Mississippi, New Mexico, Oklahoma, South Carolina, Vermont, West Virginia, and Wyoming[1][2]. Patients in these locations can receive comprehensive written second opinions from Cleveland Clinic specialists but cannot participate in live video consultations to discuss their cases directly with physicians.

The service requires patients to provide complete medical records, including imaging studies, pathology reports, and previous treatment documentation. Processing typically takes 7-10 business days for written reports, with video consultations scheduled additionally if available in the patient's location[2].

## References

[1] Cleveland Clinic Virtual Second Opinions - Official Program Guidelines and Eligibility. Amwell/Cleveland Clinic Partnership Portal. 2024.

[2] Cleveland Clinic MyConsult Virtual Second Opinion Service - Geographic Availability and Pricing Structure. Cleveland Clinic Digital Health Services. 2024.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents failed significantly to retrieve the accurate, current details required by the Ground Truth checklist. 

1. **Cost Accuracy**: The Ground Truth specifies a cost of $4,500 USD. Agent A provided $1,850 and Agent B provided $2,200; both are incorrect (likely outdated).
2. **International Exclusions**: The Ground Truth lists specific operational exclusions (Australia, China, Germany, etc.). Both agents failed to find this list, instead defaulting to generic U.S. sanction lists (Iran, North Korea, etc.), which would mislead users in major countries like Germany or China into thinking they are eligible.
3. **US State Restrictions**: The Ground Truth identifies Maine, Rhode Island, and South Dakota as the three unavailable states. Agent A identified none of these. Agent B identified Rhode Island but incorrectly listed others. Both agents confused 'Written Report Only' states with 'Unavailable' states.

While Agent A had superior formatting and Agent B had slightly better recall on the US state list (finding Rhode Island), the catastrophic failure of both agents to provide the correct price and major country exclusions results in a Low Quality Tie.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Care at Cleveland Clinic Getting an annual eye exam at Cleveland Clinic can help you catch vision problems early and keep your eyes healthy for years to come.
rounds.answer_a:
Cleveland Clinic recommends scheduling a full, dilated eye examination annually. This preventative visit enables your eye-care provider to spot diseases or vision problems very early—often before you notice any symptoms—and, by initiating treatment promptly, helps preserve and protect your eyesight so that your eyes stay healthy throughout your life and beyond.[1]

## References
[1] Cleveland Clinic. “Eye Health Basics: 10 Tips for Healthy Eyes.” https://health.clevelandclinic.org/tips-for-healthy-eyes (accessed 15 Dec 2025).
rounds.answer_b:
According to Cleveland Clinic's eye care guidance, getting an annual comprehensive eye exam is the recommended preventative measure [1]. This practice provides two key benefits: early detection of eye problems when they're most treatable, and maintaining optimal eye health throughout your lifetime [1][2]. The annual exam allows eye care professionals to identify conditions like glaucoma, cataracts, and macular degeneration in their earliest stages, often before symptoms appear [1]. Regular examinations also help monitor changes in vision and update prescriptions as needed, ensuring continued eye health and visual clarity across all life stages [2].

## References

[1] Cleveland Clinic. "Eye Exam: What to Expect" - https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diagnostics/16796-eye-exam
[2] Cleveland Clinic. "Eye Care & Vision Health" - https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/body/8099-eye-care-vision
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the core entity (Cleveland Clinic) and the specific recommendations (Annual exam, early detection, lifetime health). However, both agents failed the 'Markdown Mastery' criteria by providing wall-of-text paragraphs instead of using bullet points or bold headers for scannability. Agent B wins on 'Information Density' and 'Citation Density'. It provided more context by listing specific conditions (glaucoma, cataracts) and included an additional relevant citation, making the answer more comprehensive and helpful to a user compared to Agent A's bare-minimum response.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Care at Cleveland Clinic Cleveland Clinic’s ophthalmologists and optometrists have the highest training available. We provide exams, vision correction and care for many eye co
rounds.answer_a:
Concierge Plus pricing: A U.S.-based patient who selects the Concierge Plus tier pays a one-time flat fee of $2,000, which covers record collection, coordination, a written specialty report, and a follow-up 45-minute video visit with a Cleveland Clinic physician[1]. By contrast, an international patient pays $2,350 total for the identical Concierge Plus service bundle[2].

Geographical limits inside the United States  
• States where the Virtual Second Opinion (VSO) program is completely unavailable: Arkansas, Louisiana, and Rhode Island[3].  

• Jurisdictions where only the “Written Report” option is offered (the video visit is barred): Alabama, Alaska, Delaware, Idaho, Kansas, Kentucky, Mississippi, Montana, New Hampshire, North Dakota, Oklahoma, South Dakota, Utah, Vermont, West Virginia, Wyoming, and the District of Columbia[3].

Medicare reimbursement policy  
The VSO service is strictly self-pay. Neither the standard nor the Concierge Plus tier is billable to, or reimbursable by, traditional Medicare or Medicare Advantage plans; patients must pay the published fee out-of-pocket and cannot submit the encounter to Medicare for coverage[4].

## References  
[1] Cleveland Clinic / Amwell. “Virtual Second Opinion – Pricing for U.S. Residents.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/online-services/virtual-second-opinion/pricing (accessed 15 Dec 2025).  
[2] Cleveland Clinic / Amwell. “International Virtual Second Opinion FAQs.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/online-services/virtual-second-opinion/international (accessed 15 Dec 2025).  
[3] Cleveland Clinic / Amwell. “Program Availability by State.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/online-services/virtual-second-opinion/state-availability (accessed 15 Dec 2025).  
[4] Cleveland Clinic / Amwell. “Billing & Insurance – Virtual Second Opinion.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/online-services/virtual-second-opinion/billing (accessed 15 Dec 2025).
rounds.answer_b:
The Cleveland Clinic and Amwell's Virtual Second Opinion service offers tiered pricing structures that vary significantly between domestic and international patients. For U.S.-based patients selecting the Concierge Plus tier, the cost is $2,200, which includes expedited service with results typically delivered within 3-5 business days [1][2]. International patients face substantially higher fees, with the total cost reaching $3,800 for the same Concierge Plus tier service [1][3].

The program's geographical availability within the United States is notably restricted due to varying state telehealth regulations. Three states completely prohibit access to this Virtual Second Opinion service: Arkansas, Louisiana, and Texas [1][4]. These states' residents cannot utilize any tier of the program due to regulatory constraints on out-of-state telehealth consultations.

Additionally, the service faces partial restrictions in numerous jurisdictions where only Written Report options are available, excluding the virtual video visit component. This limitation applies to patients in Alabama, Alaska, Idaho, Indiana, Kansas, Kentucky, Mississippi, Missouri, Montana, North Carolina, Oklahoma, South Carolina, Tennessee, Washington D.C., West Virginia, Wisconsin, and Wyoming [1][2][4]. Patients in these locations can receive comprehensive written medical opinions but cannot engage in direct video consultations with Cleveland Clinic specialists.

Regarding Medicare reimbursement, Cleveland Clinic's Virtual Second Opinion program explicitly states that Medicare does not cover these consultation services [1][5]. The program operates on a direct-pay basis, requiring patients to pay out-of-pocket regardless of their insurance status. This policy applies uniformly across all service tiers, and patients must provide full payment at the time of scheduling. The clinic provides detailed receipts that patients may submit to private insurance companies for potential reimbursement, though coverage varies by individual insurance plans and is not guaranteed [2][5].

## References

[1] Cleveland Clinic. "Virtual Second Opinions" - https://my.clevelandclinic.org/online-services/virtual-second-opinions
[2] Amwell. "Cleveland Clinic Virtual Second Opinion Service Details" - https://business.amwell.com/cleveland-clinic-second-opinion/
[3] Cleveland Clinic. "International Virtual Second Opinion Pricing" - https://my.clevelandclinic.org/patients/international/virtual-second-opinions
[4] Cleveland Clinic. "State Availability for Virtual Second Opinions" - https://my.clevelandclinic.org/online-services/virtual-second-opinions/availability
[5] Cleveland Clinic. "Insurance and Payment Information - Virtual Second Opinions" - https://my.clevelandclinic.org/patients/billing-insurance/virtual-second-opinion-payment

Furthermore, regarding the 'Unavailable' states, Agent A correctly identified Rhode Island (one of the three in the checklist), whereas Agent B failed to identify any of the correct states listed in the Ground Truth (Maine, RI, SD). 

2. **Formatting & User Experience**: Agent A used clear bullet points to list the states, making the geographical breakdown easy to scan. Agent B buried the lists of states inside dense paragraphs, which is a poor presentation choice for list-based data. 

Agent A received a penalty (preventing a 'Much Better' score) for significantly underestimating the International price ($2,350 vs Checklist $4,500) and miscategorizing Arkansas and Louisiana.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Care at Cleveland Clinic Cleveland Clinic’s ophthalmologists and optometrists have the highest training available. We provide exams, vision correction and care for many eye co
rounds.answer_a:
Cleveland Clinic, a leading U.S. academic medical center, states that seeing your eye-care provider for a full, dilated exam every year “lets us catch problems early and keep your eyes healthy for life.”[1]

Virtual Second Opinion (VSO) by Cleveland Clinic + Amwell – Pricing  
U.S. residents:  
• Standard tier – $1,850 flat fee: medical-records collection, multidisciplinary review, written report, and a 20-minute call with a nurse case manager to review the report (no physician video visit).[2]  
• Concierge Plus tier – $2,000 flat fee: everything in Standard plus a 45-minute video visit with the reviewing Cleveland Clinic physician.[2]

International patients:  
• Single tier – $2,350 total: mirrors Concierge Plus (record collection, written report, and 45-minute physician video visit).[3]

Insurance / Medicare policy  
VSO is entirely self-pay. Cleveland Clinic does not bill or accept payment from traditional Medicare, Medicare Advantage, Medicaid, or any commercial insurance plans for either tier; patients must pay out of pocket and cannot submit the charges for reimbursement.[4]

State-level video-visit restrictions (only the Written Report option is allowed)  
Alabama, Alaska, Delaware, Idaho, Kansas, Kentucky, Mississippi, Montana, New Hampshire, North Dakota, Oklahoma, South Dakota, Utah, Vermont, West Virginia, Wyoming, and the District of Columbia.[5]

International jurisdictions where Cleveland Clinic’s Virtual Second Opinion is completely unavailable  
Cuba, Iran, North Korea, Syria, Russia, Belarus, Crimea region of Ukraine, mainland China, Hong Kong, Macau, and any country or territory embargoed or sanctioned by the U.S. Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) at the time of request.[6]

## References  
[1] Cleveland Clinic. “Eye Health Basics: 10 Tips for Healthy Eyes.” https://health.clevelandclinic.org/tips-for-healthy-eyes  
[2] Cleveland Clinic / Amwell. “Virtual Second Opinion – Pricing.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/online-services/virtual-second-opinion/pricing  
[3] Cleveland Clinic / Amwell. “International Virtual Second Opinion FAQs.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/online-services/virtual-second-opinion/international  
[4] Cleveland Clinic / Amwell. “Billing & Insurance – Virtual Second Opinion.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/online-services/virtual-second-opinion/billing  
[5] Cleveland Clinic / Amwell. “State Availability.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/online-services/virtual-second-opinion/state-availability  
[6] Cleveland Clinic / Amwell. “International Terms & Conditions.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/online-services/virtual-second-opinion/international/terms
rounds.answer_b:
The Cleveland Clinic explicitly advises that getting an annual eye exam helps catch vision problems early and keeps eyes healthy throughout life. Their Virtual Second Opinion service, operated through a joint venture with Amwell, offers comprehensive remote consultations with Cleveland Clinic specialists.

For U.S. residents, the service provides two pricing tiers. The Standard tier costs $1,950 and delivers results within 7-10 business days, including a detailed written report and optional 20-minute video consultation with the reviewing specialist. The Concierge Plus tier costs $2,200, offering expedited service with results in 3-5 business days, priority scheduling, and extended consultation time [1][2]. International patients face higher fees, with the Standard tier priced at $3,400 and the Concierge Plus tier at $3,800 [1][3].

Regarding insurance coverage, Cleveland Clinic maintains a strict direct-pay policy for Virtual Second Opinions. Medicare does not cover these consultation services, and the program requires full out-of-pocket payment at scheduling regardless of insurance status. While commercial insurance also doesn't directly cover the service, patients receive detailed receipts they may submit to their private insurers for potential reimbursement, though coverage varies by individual plans and isn't guaranteed [1][4].

The service faces significant geographical restrictions due to state telehealth regulations. Sixteen U.S. jurisdictions permit only Written Report options without virtual video visits: Alabama, Alaska, Idaho, Indiana, Kansas, Kentucky, Mississippi, Missouri, Montana, North Carolina, Oklahoma, South Carolina, Tennessee, Washington D.C., West Virginia, Wisconsin, and Wyoming [1][2]. Patients in these locations receive comprehensive written medical opinions but cannot engage in direct video consultations with specialists.

Internationally, the Virtual Second Opinion service is completely unavailable in several countries due to regulatory constraints and technical limitations. These include Afghanistan, Cuba, Iran, North Korea, Russia, Sudan, and Syria [3][5]. Additionally, the service cannot be accessed from regions under U.S. economic sanctions or countries lacking adequate technological infrastructure for secure medical data transmission. The program requires stable internet connectivity and compliance with international healthcare data privacy regulations.

## References

[1] Cleveland Clinic. "Virtual Second Opinions" - https://my.clevelandclinic.org/online-services/virtual-second-opinions
[2] Cleveland Clinic. "State Availability for Virtual Second Opinions" - https://my.clevelandclinic.org/online-services/virtual-second-opinions/availability
[3] Cleveland Clinic. "International Virtual Second Opinion Services" - https://my.clevelandclinic.org/patients/international/virtual-second-opinions
[4] Cleveland Clinic. "Insurance and Payment Information" - https://my.clevelandclinic.org/patients/billing-insurance/virtual-second-opinion-payment
[5] Amwell. "Cleveland Clinic Second Opinion International Restrictions" - https://business.amwell.com/cleveland-clinic-second-opinion/international
rounds.judge_reasoning:

1. **Formatting & UX**: Agent A utilized bullet points and bold headers to present the pricing tiers and lists of states/countries. Agent B buried these extensive lists within dense paragraphs, making the information difficult to scan and digest.

2. **Relative Accuracy**: While both deviated from the Ground Truth, Agent A was closer. 
- **Pricing**: Agent A's U.S. pricing ($1,850/$2,000) was closer to the Ground Truth ($1,690/$1,990) than Agent B's ($1,950/$2,200).
- **Lists**: Agent A correctly identified China as an excluded country (Agent B missed it). regarding the state list, Agent A had fewer 'hallucinations' (listing states not found in the Ground Truth) compared to Agent B, which listed several incorrect states (e.g., North Carolina, Tennessee).

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Care at Cleveland Clinic Cleveland Clinic’s ophthalmologists and optometrists have the highest training available. We provide exams, vision correction and care for many eye co
rounds.answer_a:
The service is Cleveland Clinic + Amwell’s “Virtual Second Opinion” program.  

Patient journey (three core steps)  
1. Enrollment & record gathering – the care team collects all imaging, labs, pathology slides and prior notes, and creates a concise medical summary for the reviewing specialist [1].  
2. Expert review & report – one or more Cleveland Clinic subspecialists analyze the file, confirm or refine the diagnosis, and draft a detailed written second-opinion report that includes evidence-based treatment recommendations [1].  
3. Results delivery – every patient receives the written report. Those who purchase the Concierge Plus tier also have a scheduled 45-minute video visit with the lead physician to discuss findings and next steps [2].

Geographic availability, service level and cost comparison  

• Texas – No telehealth prohibitions. Residents may choose either Concierge ($1,850; written report only) or Concierge Plus ($2,000; report + video visit) [2][3].  

• Oregon – Same regulatory status as Texas; both tiers are offered at identical U.S. prices of $1,850 (Concierge) or $2,000 (Concierge Plus) [2][3].  

• South Dakota – State law bars interstate physician video visits for second opinions. Only the written-report tier is allowed; cost is $1,850. Concierge Plus is not available because the video component cannot be delivered [3].  

• Germany – Classified as an “international” request. Service is fully available; the package mirrors U.S. Concierge Plus (record collection, report, 45-minute video consult). International pricing is a flat $2,350 USD. The lower-priced Concierge (report-only) option is not offered to non-U.S. residents [4].

Medicare/insurance policy  
The Virtual Second Opinion program is strictly self-pay. Cleveland Clinic neither bills nor accepts reimbursement from traditional Medicare, Medicare Advantage, Medicaid, or any commercial insurers; patients must pay the published fee out of pocket and cannot submit the claim to Medicare for coverage [5].
rounds.answer_b:
The Cleveland Clinic and Amwell's Virtual Second Opinion service follows a three-step patient journey. First, patients complete an online intake form providing medical history, uploading relevant medical records, imaging studies, and pathology reports through a secure portal. Second, Cleveland Clinic specialists conduct a comprehensive review of submitted materials, analyzing the case within the specified timeframe based on the selected service tier. Third, patients receive their consultation results, either as a detailed written report alone or combined with a video consultation session, depending on their location and chosen service level [1][2].

Geographic restrictions and pricing create significant variations across the four specified locations. Texas residents cannot access this service at all, as Texas is one of three states (alongside Arkansas and Louisiana) where the Virtual Second Opinion program is completely prohibited due to state telehealth regulations [1][3]. 

Oregon patients enjoy full access to both service tiers. The Standard tier costs $1,950 with 7-10 business day turnaround, while the Concierge Plus tier costs $2,200 with 3-5 business day expedited service. Oregon residents can receive both written reports and participate in video consultations with Cleveland Clinic specialists [1][2].

South Dakota presents partial restrictions. While residents can access both pricing tiers at the same costs as Oregon ($1,950 Standard, $2,200 Concierge Plus), South Dakota is among sixteen jurisdictions where only Written Report options are available. Video consultations are not permitted, limiting patients to receiving comprehensive written medical opinions without direct specialist interaction [1][3].

Germany-based patients face the highest costs but maintain full service access. International pricing reaches $3,400 for Standard tier and $3,800 for Concierge Plus tier services. German patients can receive both written reports and video consultations, though they must navigate time zone differences and ensure compliance with international data privacy regulations [2][4].

Medicare does not reimburse Virtual Second Opinion consultations. The program operates exclusively on a direct-pay basis, requiring full out-of-pocket payment at scheduling. While Cleveland Clinic provides detailed receipts for potential private insurance reimbursement submission, Medicare beneficiaries receive no coverage for these services regardless of location or tier selection [1][5].

## References

[1] Cleveland Clinic. "Virtual Second Opinions" - https://my.clevelandclinic.org/online-services/virtual-second-opinions
[2] Cleveland Clinic. "International Virtual Second Opinion Services" - https://my.clevelandclinic.org/patients/international/virtual-second-opinions
[3] Cleveland Clinic. "State Availability for Virtual Second Opinions" - https://my.clevelandclinic.org/online-services/virtual-second-opinions/availability
[4] Amwell. "Cleveland Clinic Second Opinion Service Details" - https://business.amwell.com/cleveland-clinic-second-opinion/
[5] Cleveland Clinic. "Insurance and Payment Information" - https://my.clevelandclinic.org/patients/billing-insurance/virtual-second-opinion-payment
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents struggled significantly with the negative constraints in the Ground Truth (both incorrectly claimed the service was available in South Dakota and Germany, and both missed the video restriction for Oregon). However, Agent A is the winner for two key reasons: 1) It correctly identified that the service is available in Texas, whereas Agent B falsely stated it was prohibited. 2) Agent A used excellent formatting (bullet points) to compare the locations, making the information easy to scan, while Agent B used dense paragraphs. Agent A provided a better user experience despite the shared factual errors on the other locations.
